title: Making sprig moulds from found objects
subtitle: Pressing clay into found objects to make reusable sprig decoration moulds
excerpt: A technique for making small press moulds from found objects (coins, buttons, shells, carved erasers) and using them to cast repeating clay sprigs for decoration. Sprigs are pressed out, allowed to stiffen slightly, and attached to a clay form with score and slip.
sourceNotes: Sprig decoration using press moulds is found in European stoneware and salt-glaze traditions from the seventeenth century onward. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A small relief clay shape pressed from a mould and applied to a ceramic surface as decoration.
Scratching joining surfaces and applying clay slurry before pressing together. Required when attaching sprigs to leather-hard clay.
The drying stage where clay holds its shape. Sprigs are applied at leather-hard for the best adhesion.

content.text: A
sprig

is a small relief clay shape pressed from a mould and applied as surface decoration. The mould can be any object with a raised or recessed surface: a coin, a carved rubber eraser, a small shell, a carved plaster tile, or a button. This tutorial covers making moulds from found objects and using them to produce sprigs for applying to coil pots, slab dishes, and hand-built forms.
Choosing objects for mould-making

Good sprig mould sources have clean relief detail at least 1-2 mm deep. Shallow relief produces weak sprigs that flatten during application. Good sources include: stamped coins (deeper relief transfers well), decorative buttons with raised motifs, textured shells with clear patterns, carved rubber erasers, small plaster tile offcuts. Objects with undercuts (where the mould locks into the clay) should be avoided; a simple relief with no reverse geometry releases cleanly.
Making the air-dry clay mould
Press a small ball of air-dry clay (about 30 g) over the object face, pressing firmly into every detail.
Allow the clay to stiffen to leather-hard (30-60 minutes) before peeling the mould away. At leather-hard, the clay holds the impression without distorting on removal.
Set the mould face-up to dry fully to bone-dry. Once bone-dry, seal the mould face with two coats of PVA varnish to prevent it from sticking to fresh clay in use.
Pressing sprigs from the mould
Lightly dust the sealed mould face with a small amount of dry clay powder or powdered chalk as a release agent. This prevents the fresh clay from sticking.
Press a small ball of fresh clay firmly into the mould face, filling all the recesses.
Trim the excess clay from the back of the mould flush with the needle tool. The back should be flat.
Allow the pressed sprig to stiffen slightly in the mould (10-15 minutes), then flex the mould gently to release the sprig. It should pop free cleanly.
Attaching sprigs to a form
Sprigs should be applied when both the sprig and the form are at
leather-hard
Score and slip

both the back of the sprig and the corresponding surface on the form. Press the sprig firmly into place and hold for a slow count of fifteen. Run the needle tool around the edge of the sprig to press the join closed. Allow the join to firm up before handling.
Common mistakes
Releasing the sprig from the mould too soon, while still soft. The sprig deforms on removal.
Applying a soft sprig to a leather-hard form without score and slip. The join has no mechanical grip and the sprig falls off during drying.
Not sealing the mould before use. Unsealed air-dry clay moulds absorb moisture from fresh clay and lock the sprig in place.
